original_abstract:
The African Independent Churches Association (AICA) occupies a distinctive position within the landscape of South African theological education. Driven by a commitment to intellectual growth and doctrinal integrity, the African Independent Churches (AICs) have long prioritized the formal training of their leadership to ensure that their theological foundations remain deeply connected to the lived experiences of their congregants. While the AICA initially established its own theological college, it eventually entered into landmark negotiations with the Umphumulo Lutheran Theological College (ULTC) to facilitate the education of its students. 

This article investigates the motivations behind the AICs' decision to train their ministry candidates at the ULTC and examines the broader implications of this ecumenical partnership. The study is structured into four key sections: first, it explores the early collaborative relationship between the ULTC and the AICs; second, it analyzes why these independent churches sought out established mainline theological institutions for leadership development; third, it reflects on the founding, successes, and various institutional hurdles faced by the AICA Theological College; and finally, it scrutinizes the curricular tensions and pedagogical challenges encountered by AIC students, alongside the supportive initiatives provided by the Lutheran community. Ultimately, this research highlights the vital role of theological education in empowering pastoral leadership to serve indigenous African communities effectively.
humanized_abstract:
The African Independent Churches Association (AICA) is one of the numerous associations that are found in the pluralistic context of South African theological education. The African Independent Churches (AICs) have from the inception championed the intellectual development and theological doctrine with fervour. For this reason they have continually emphasised the formal training of their clergy to ensure that they are equipped with a theology that is connected with the experiences of their church members. AICA, has its own Theological College but has, since a few years ago, embarked upon a pioneering exercise, which has so far borne fruit, through an affiliation with the Umphumulo Lutheran Theological College (ULTC) for the training of future AICA ministers.

This paper examines the reasons behind the AICs’ decision to train their candidates at the ULTC and the implications of this ecumenical venture. The paper is organised into four sections. The first section examines the beginnings of the partnership between the ULTC and the AICs. The second section examines the reasons behind the AICs’ decision to train their future leaders at mainline Protestant theological colleges. The third section describes the formation, achievements and challenges of the AICA Theological College. The fourth section reflects on the challenges students from the AICs faced in terms of the content and methodology of their studies as well as the positive aspects of the Lutheran involvement in the ULTC. This paper contributes to understanding the importance of theological education for the equipping of African Church leaders for their pastoral ministry to the people of Africa.
